Yeah, I've managed to make some tests pass by relaxing them to only test the desired end result without testing the actual order of execution. Here's and example:

```python

def grouper(n, iterable, fillvalue=None):
    """"grouper(3, 'ABCDEFG', 'x') --> ABC DEF Gxx"""
    args = [iter(iterable)] * n
    return zip_longest(fillvalue=fillvalue, *args)

class TestLock:
    def test_lock_sequence(self, kernel):
        results = []

        async def worker(lck, label):
            async with lck:
                results.append((label, ' acquire'))
                await sleep(0.25)
                results.append((label, ' releasing'))

        workers = [1, 2, 3]

        async def main():
            lck = Lock()
            for i in workers:
                await spawn(worker(lck, i))

        kernel.run(main())

        assert len(results) == 2 * len(workers), "all workers should complete their tasks"

        # each chunk represents a set of actions inside a critical section
        chunks = utils.grouper(2, results)

        # list of sets with unique worker ids per chunk
        ids = [set([i[0] for i in chunk]) for chunk in chunks]

        # check whether each chunk contains only actions from a single
        # worker task, which will prove that at any point in time only
        # one worker was acting in a critical section
        assert all(
            [len(s) == 1 for s in ids]
        ), "only a single worker should act in a critical section"
```
I think this makes test less explicit, but makes it more generic. 

My main point is that it would be nice to have generic tests, so we can play around with different schedulers without writing separate tests for them. I could also add only a subset of tests, that depend on the scheduler, and keep current tests as is.

(NOTE: still a work-in-progress)

basically, this is changing around the structure so that the main glom callback is a very simple dispatch loop:

```python
    def _glom(self, target, spec, context):
        # TODO: de-recursivize this
        # TODO: rearrange the branching below by frequency of use
        # recursive self._glom() calls should pass path=path to elide the current
        # step, otherwise add the current spec in some fashion
        context = context.new_child({})
        for type_, handler in self._spec_registry.items():
            if type_ is callable and callable(spec):
                return handler(spec, target, context)
            if type_ is not callable and isinstance(spec, type_):
                return handler(spec, target, context)
```

currently, the handlers dispatch table is an instance of the glommer; but, there's nothing stopping us from making it a part of context; if we did that, then you could have completely disjoint gloms pasted together into one big glom (by overriding the dispatch tables & etc in context for the sub-tree that wants to play by different rules)

here's what that dispatch table looks like currently:

```python
_DEFAULT_SPEC_REGISTRY = OrderedDict([
    (Inspect, Inspect._handler),
    (dict, _handle_dict),
    (list, _handle_list),
    (tuple, _handle_tuple),
    (_TType, _t_eval),  # NOTE: must come before callable b/c T is also callable
    (Call, Call._handler),
    (callable, lambda s, t, c: s(t)),
])
```

here's a very simple (but non-trivial) call-back for handling tuples:

```python
def _handle_tuple(spec, target, context):
    res = target
    for subspec in spec:
        res = context[HANDLE_CHILD](res, subspec, context)
        if not isinstance(subspec, list):
            context[Path] += [getattr(subspec, '__name__', subspec)]
    return res
```

here's the most complex one, the callback for `Inspect` -- it takes two new methods of the `Inspect` class to implement

(note, the Inspect code is possible to be separated out rather than scattered here and there)
```python
    def _handler(self, target, context):
        # stash the real handler under Inspect,
        # and replace the child handler with a trace callback
        context[Inspect] = context[HANDLE_CHILD]
        context[HANDLE_CHILD] = self._trace
        return context[HANDLE_CHILD](target, self.wrapped, context)

    def _trace(self, target, spec, context):
        if not self.recursive:
            context[HANDLE_CHILD] = context[Inspect]
        if self.echo:
            print('---')
            print('path:  ', context[Path] + [spec])
            print('target:', target)
        if self.breakpoint:
            self.breakpoint()
        try:
            ret = context[Inspect](target, self.wrapped, context)
        except Exception:
            if self.post_mortem:
                self.post_mortem()
            raise
        if self.echo:
            print('output:', ret)
            print('---')
        return ret
```

> […] does TheFuck first check for a none-zero exit command?

Not exactly. It runs the command regardless of the exit status. Its interest is in the command's output ( both `STDERR` and `STDOUT`).

So, imagine you hit `open index.html`. `open` does not fail here. Instead, it successfully opens `index.html` with the default web browser. Assuming TheFuck could fix that and turn it into `open -e index.html`, you then think *“Fuck!”*, close the browser and hit `fuck`. TheFuck then runs your previous command – `open` – to get its output, which in case loads up `index.html` with the default web browser yet again and exits right away, flawlessly, with no output. With that info, TheFuck prompts you with the *fixed* command, but you're left with `index.html` opened in the default web browser.

Perhaps some rules could try and match the command script alone, regardlessly of the output, and TheFuck could run a first round of those rules before running the command itself. It could also base on the last command's exit status. This way we would assume the user knows what he's trying to unfuck. Not really sure how well this could work, though, I haven't thought much about it.

This is actually a feature of toml-0.4. Keys may not be enclosed by single quotes. See https://github.com/toml-lang/toml/blob/master/versions/en/toml-v0.4.0.md#table, where the keys follow the rules for "basic strings", and basic strings are always double-quoted.

The master version of toml changed that and now allowes "literal strings" as well.

I'm not going to support new features in toml, but if you want to submit a pull-request, I'm willing to merge it.

python3 treats any directory as a package. Because you have a directory called noise, when you call `import noise`. It imports the directory in the current working directory before looking in site-packages (causing this error). You can cd to another directory and `import noise` or move the noise git directory to prevent this module name collision

There is a an issue here. The add and remove functions are using the following:

`StatementId=function.name+'_'+self._context.environment`

This means that you can only add one SNS event source. On the second attempt you will get an error as you are using the same statement ID:

`kappa.event_source.sns - DEBUG - Permission already exists - Continuing`

Instead of using the function name, you could use the event source name which should be unique e.g.

`StatementId=self.arn.split(":")[-1]`

@aquavitae 

SO I just found out that, under Python 3, the reportlab "invariant" output isn't, which may have been part of what you're struggling with.  Sure, it makes the date invariant, but for some reason, everything else moves around in the output PDF file.

For my tests, the fix was simple -- since I work very hard to keep pdfrw's output invariant, and since I only cared about how the file is viewed (and not the metadata), after I output the files from reportlab, I run them through pdfrw again, and then I achieve the invariance.  The pdfrw code to do this is very simple:

from pdfrw import PdfReader, PdfWriter

PdfWriter().addpages(PdfReader(reportlab_filename).pages).write(final_filename)

             elif self.mode == IP_V6_ONLY:

@@ -159,7 +159,7 @@
         try:
             if self.connection.server.connect_timeout:
                 self.connection.socket.settimeout(self.connection.server.connect_timeout)
-            self.connection.socket.connect(self.connection.server.address_info[0][4])

             if self.connection.server.connect_timeout:
                 self.connection.socket.settimeout(None)  # disable socket timeout - socket is in blocking mode

Thanks for reporting. This function was originally written to get introns when only exons exist. In that case, it made sense to ensure strands were the same but when getting intergenic regions, it's overly restrictive. There's also a constraint that the chromosomes must match.

The longer-term solution is to set an inter-feature's strand to be `"."` when the flanking features are on different strands, and to handle multiple chromosomes correctly. I'm assuming you don't want to use `bedtools complement` since there's not a straightforward way of getting flanking genes for each intergenic region.

The current workaround would be the following, where you manually reset the gene strand and only call `interfeatures` on one chromosome at a time:

```python
chroms = [i['seqid'] for i in db.execute('SELECT DISTINCT seqid FROM features;')]

def genes_on_chrom(chrom):
    """
    Yield genes on `chrom`, sorted by start position
    """
    for g in db.features_of_type('gene', order_by='start', limit=(chrom, 0, 1e9))
        g.strand = '.'
        yield g

def intergenic():
    """
    Yield intergenic features
    """
    for chrom in chroms:
        genes = genes_on_chrom(chrom)
        for intergenic in db.interfeatures(genes):
            yield intergenic
```

My solution only hides exceptions raised by the Handler. Since this is executed in a thread, the main program does not have access to handling the exception and the program will continue to work despite raised exceptions. This is why I suggest to hide the exceptions from the Handler so that console output is not cluttered by exceptions that can not be handled anyways. Instead, you can still log the error message with the Python logging module as you suggest, which can be configured to write to stdout, a file, etc.

The `logging.raiseExceptions = False` was provided for completeness, since a console message is otherwise still displayed by the `logging` module.

It is not merely required that the systems be linear. It is also required that the Markov property exists (that state X_k depends solely on state x_k-1), and that the noise is white. I.e, there cannot be autocorrelation between states. In this case the states are autocorrelated, and the noise is not white due to the exogenous shock. The plain KF will not perform well. I think we are coming to opposite conclusions; I don't think this is a bug, I think this is the UKF appropriately performing differently in the face of autocorrelation, lack of the Markov property, and non-white noise. 

Please understand I am "babbling" - this is not my area. Wikipedia to the rescue:

> or an AR(1) process with a positive {\displaystyle \varphi } \varphi , only the previous term in the process and the noise term contribute to the output. If {\displaystyle \varphi } \varphi  is close to 0, then the process still looks like white noise, but as {\displaystyle \varphi } \varphi  approaches 1, the output gets a larger contribution from the previous term relative to the noise. This results in a "smoothing" or integration of the output, similar to a low pass filter.

and

> Because each shock affects X values infinitely far into the future from when they occur, any given value Xt is affected by shocks occurring infinitely far into the past. 

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autoregressive_model

This is unfortunately not very well covered in most of the literature; I'm digging through some books.

Note that is more or less unreadable to me, other than to note that the shocks pose considerable difficulties with no clear answers. I have no idea (!) if this applies to your simple example.

I do not eyeball any errors, but I suppose I should write some tests to better ensure I am happy with my implementation. To answer the first question, yes its possible to do this, in fact it is default behavior. The default pattern for Kafka publishing does not duplicate messages. The default partition strategy is random so when you send a message it will get sent to a random partition. You should not have to do anything special to achieve your requirement.

I would recommend setting up a tighter test to narrow down where the issue is.

- For now create a new topic for each test. I assume you have topic auto creation on so this should not be a problem. 
- In your message include a timestamp or perhaps a uuid so you can 100% positive that the messages are really duplicated.
- In the test setup maybe send a defined number of messages greater than 1. Then on consumption count the number of messages that are returned.

Thank you. I see that your code is attempting a fast executemany() after it has already used the cursor for some other execution, and the (ODBC, not pyODBC) cursor has not been closed. execute() and regular executemany() closes the cursor first, but the fast executemany implementation does not.

As a workaround, you can consume all the resultsets first, which will automatically close the cursor:
```
while cursor.nextset():
    pass
```
(This is recommended in general, especially in the case of statements which return multiple resultsets/rowcounts, [because failure to do so causes other problems](https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/issues/277).)

**NOTE:** I am referring to the ODBC cursor throughout; this is unrelated to and not solved by using the cursor.close() in Python, since that not only closes but _frees_ the underlying ODBC statement handle.

 /usr/bin/env python -c "import matplotlib; print matplotlib.__version__"

Some further notes on this for when we get back to it. This behavior is evident in the PowerPoint UI. Just create a default column chart and delete the 2nd and 3rd series and you'll see it. The "auto-title" appears containing the name of the first series.

In the UI you can delete the chart title, which adds the following element to the XML:

```xml
  <c:chart>
    <c:autoTitleDeleted val="1"/>  <!-- this one -->
    <c:plotArea>
    ...
```

..after which the chart expands vertically to take up the space formerly occupied by the chart title. So apparently this behavior is called "Auto-title" and is very much on purpose.

It appears that if you _really_ want to get rid of the chart title, you need to add this element as well as getting rid of the title element.

Thank you for consulting us before committing changes. Here is my feedback for you:

1. I don't know the specifications for MDF v2 and v3 so my advices are more for the reflection:
It is of course good if you can refactor the code into one class if you know that the code is really most of the same. However, if it implies to put lots of conditions (if) in the code, it could also become very confusing especially for new contributors but I think you already thought about this.
Otherwise for the filename, if you use mdf_v2_v3, you can rename mdf4 to mdf_v4 if it makes things more consistent. You can also keep two files mdf2 and mdf3, and make MDF3 inherits from MDF2 class, it's interesting especially if MDF3 is more of an MDF2 extension and if you feel like that you will have different implementations for certain things between MDF2 and MDF3 (but since you feel like merging, maybe it's not the case).

2.
    1. to be honest, I don't like this configure thing.
    I would rather create two iterator functions: itersignals() and itergroups() just like itervalues() and iterkeys() for dictionaries. And I would then choose itersignals() as the default one for the `for signal in mdf:`.
    IMO, it is more sustainable, you can easily add new iterators in the future, and you can even change the default iterator because itersignals() and itergroups() will always remain.

    2. I would yield master channels as well. There are signals like every other channels.

@pawapps The "nonce window" part would make a good separate issue. ;)

As to network connection reliability:

`v1` didn't gracefully handle the case where the remote "dropped" the connection between queries. You'd have to check the connection state in advance, prior to sending the query; or reset the connection manually and repeat the failed query. The latter, when done inappropriately, could also result in _two_ open connections, or at least a `Connection` object floating in memory... All of this is too much networking where people are usually concerned with business logic (often literally).

In `v2`, `requests` handles this behind the scene. So you'll "only" get a `requests` exception as a direct result of performing a query; as compared to something that _looks_ like a failed query, but didn't even make it to CloudFlare.

The latter is a rare case these days, which this issue is about.

What you just described is't a behavior of squash tool, but Docker itself and I'll prove it, but first - some history.

With the move to format v2 of the container image (Docker 1.10 or newer), Docker made thing way more complicated compared to v1 where layer ID's (what you are expecting in the `docker history` output) were just the ID's of the layer. Now this is not true anymore for a reason I do not understand fully. You may be interested in this: https://github.com/goldmann/docker-squash/issues/48 for some history about these findings and 1.10+ support.

BTW, specifying number of layers to squash is exactly about working around this issue - in case you have missing layers - you can specify number of layers to squash.

Now, to prove that this is not a squash issue, consider following (familiar) Dockerfile:

Hi Bob,
Thank you for the concerns you have raised. It has been discussed before in issue https://github.com/taspinar/twitterscraper/issues/60 but because the legality of scraping websites in general and twitter.com in particular is not that clear cut and strongly depends on the country the users are living in I have not done anything about it. Furthermore, as far as i know, most of the users are scraping twitter for academical and/or research purposes, without any commercial incentives. 

As it was also pointed out in issue #60 , although this package violates Twitter TOS, scraping a public website does not require people to accept the TOS. Furthermore, robots.txt allows for scraping of `/search?q=%23`, which is what twitterscraper does. The only clear violation I have seen is the time-delay of 1 sec between successive requests. 
When I have time, I'll try to implement the option to add a time-delay of 1 sec between successive requests people can turn on with a command line argument. Users for whom scraping time is not important could turn that option on.

In the mean time, feel free to sent in an PR with an added section to the readme file where this issue is explained.

Your options are to employ environment variables:

Or to wrap `stderr` before the `StreamHandler` initiates:

```python
import time
import logging

progressbar.streams.wrap_stderr()
logging.basicConfig()

bar = progressbar.ProgressBar()
for i in bar(range(10)):
    logging.error('Got %d', i)
    time.sleep(0.2)

You raise a very good point.

This decision does remove the ability to do what you want to do short of subclassing hdf5storage.MarshallerCollection and using it, which would be a pain.

I made the change because I am trying to add support for other packages to provide plugins that can provide additional marshallers and wanted a definitive loading priority to keep marshallers from different sources interfering with each other too much, and figured protecting the builtin ones would do that best.

However, after reading what you wrote, I realize that this is not the best decision. This ordering is legitimate, but so was the old one and the one you want to keep.

So, I think the best solution is to make the order configurable, to allow the user to decide what the priority order should be for user provided marshallers, builtin marshallers, and plugin marshallers. I will implement this.

Also, I probably should make it possible to get all the available marshallers and make it possible for the user to set the loading order explicitly (allow interleaving of priorities).

As for the feature you are trying to do with overloading the builtin marshaller, that is actually a good candidate for a feature that would be controlled by an option to add to the builtin marshaller. You probably aren't the only one to want such a feature. It seems like a good idea to add.

Quantization is definitely handy, which is half of why I built the iterator. The good thing about Billboard charts is that they're _always_ 7 days after the last one (even with release-date changes).
I figured it was a lot quicker to just calculate that from the start date (1958-08-09) each time than to load/parse each page - unless you can think of any math workarounds for calculating that (counting just weeks since then might be straightforward).
Right now it's not smart enough to quantize a single date alone (eg 2015-01-01 won't quantize to 2015-01-03 or 2014-12-27), but it'd be pretty easy to write that feature into it. It's probably wise to quantize _forward_ rather than backward, because billboard charts are always marked ahead of the current date (the current charts are dated July 23rd).

Wrapping the password in a HMAC is not necessarily a bad move, it does increase the attack surface in some scenarios (e.g. compromised database, but SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT is kept secure).  For other scenarios, it adds little value.

However, as implemented it's tricky to do things like migrate from legacy password schemes, as originally described in the issue.  My patch was the minimum work I could think of to support my situation, I did not have a particularly large user list to migrate.

Given backwards compatibility concerns, I think the best refactoring would be to do the following:

1.  Create a custom passlib Flask-Security password scheme that simply does the HMAC with SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT, then delegates SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH
2.  Configure Flask-Security to use this wrapper in SECURITY_PASSWORD_SCHEMES

This would allow legacy password schemes to be added to SECURITY_PASSWORD_SCHEMES, and they would receive unmolested passwords and follow the standard passlib fallback mechanisms.  Out of the box, passwords would continue to be HMAC-ed, and in-place upgrades of Flask-Security would not break existing password databases.
I have now released this fix. Let me know if this resolves the problems for you.

I just ran into the same issue today.

If you look at the `AsynchronousLogstashHandler`'s code, it *never* sets its own formatter. Therefore, the formatter of the logger is not inherited by the handler.

Try doing this hack instead:
```python
test_formatter = LogstashFormatter(message_type='abcd', tags=None, fqdn=False, extra_prefix='new', extra=None)
test_logger.handlers[0].formatter = test_formatter
```
By replacing the handler's formatter directly, you make logstash receive the correct `"type" => "abcd"`.

Essentially, you create a credential provider that subclasses from [CredentialProvider](https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/credentials.py#L424).  Then you inject it into the session:

```
cred_provider = session.get_component('credential_provider')
cred_provider.insert_before('shared-credentials-file', provider)
```

The `botocore/credentials.py` file has a lot of credential providers you can use as examples.

A good complete example would be the assume role provider that use to be in the AWS CLI: https://github.com/aws/aws-cli/blob/1.7.43/awscli/customizations/assumerole.py#L33

There's a little more code involved because it handles automatically refreshing temporary credentials.  Interestingly, this code is no longer in the AWS CLI.  It was initially only in the CLI and not botocore, but was moved over to botocore based on customer feedback.

Thanks for the pull request.  Not opposed to to this change (though we'll need to get a test added first), but I'm curious how you're able to get S3 configured with v2.

The only signature versions that are going to work with S3 are `HmacV1Auth (type='s3')` and `S3SigV4Auth (type='s3v4')`.

The `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT` is still used in two places, `_get_client_args` and the `__init__` of the Config class.  I would expected either:

* We use the values from the Config object directly in `_get_client_args`, since they have default values if they're not provided.  This will require some restructuring of the internals of `_get_client_args`.
* The `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT` is used in `_get_client_args` and has no default in `Config`.

I think I slightly prefer option 1 because I *think* the implementation ends up being simpler.

There are two places to modify to make this work:

1. the `__getitem__` method in the [cython source](https://github.com/clab/dynet/blob/master/python/_dynet.pyx#L1424-L1485). This should be pretty easy to do: just interpret the slice argument and call the according dynet `pick*` node.

2. For more efficiency we might need to create some more picking nodes (e.g. pick from multiple dimensions, pick with step size, etc) in [there](https://github.com/clab/dynet/blob/master/dynet/nodes-select.h) and corresponding expressions.

In theory everything couls be implemented with 1., so if anyone wants to do it i suggest starting there. Then later on we can add relevant nodes in the C++ code to make it more efficient.

It sounds like you're hitting a `gunicorn` limitation. Deis v1.6.1 uses [docker-registry v0.9.1 plus tweaks](https://github.com/deis/docker-registry/tree/new-repository-import-v091) with 4 worker processes, so it looks like you could use the [`GUNICORN_WORKERS`](https://github.com/deis/docker-registry/blob/new-repository-import-v091/contrib/gunicorn_config.py#L16) environment variable to increase the concurrent capacity. This would require building a custom container or modifying the systemd unit to pass that variable into the container with `-e`, but might be a workraround.

> Few questions
> 1) What happens if you add a cert with a CN that doesn't match any of the specified domains? Does it still accept it (with a warning), in the expectation you will add the custom domain later?

It will return HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST. The expectation is that the custom domain [must be present](https://github.com/deis/deis/pull/2911/files#diff-98fe74250471c27aef86277741604ebdR41) before you add the domain.

> 2) What happens if you add a cert with a CN that does match the CN from a previously uploaded cert, eg with a greater expiry date? (I guess once a cert is created is is valid and doesn't have a valid since in the future you can safely overwrite the other one)

The certificate and key will be replaced, granted that you have the correct permissions to edit the domain's certificates.

I'll introduce a breaking change to rename `--encryption` to `--encryption-services` and fix this issue in the process.

We just migrated some of our assets from S3 to wabs using a combination of

```
s3cmd sync s3://mybucket $PWD
az storage blob upload-batch -c mycontainer -s $PWD
```

And as it tuned out, our PNG images in the old S3 bucket were uploaded with content-type `application/octet-stream` as well. s3cmd (or s3 itself?) would've automatically handled this and set more sane content types for files.

Our fix to this issue was to update the metadata of the files after the fact using

```
az storage blob update -c mycontainer -n myimage.png --content-type image/png
```

Just posting for others as a workaround if they hit this as well. :)

After closer examination, what you're asking for actually doesn't solve the problem you've stated. A timeout parameter is now present on every API which makes service calls, but the timeout parameter isn't related to sockets. It applies to the service. Namely, the storage service itself will return a timeout error if it cannot complete the request in the specified amount of time.

Client side timeouts will be coming in some form or another, but like JC said there's a lot of different solutions. For example, the requests module which we use under the covers exposes a [timeout](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/quickstart/#timeouts) parameter but it's not really what you're asking for either: "timeout is not a time limit on the entire response download; rather, an exception is raised if the server has not issued a response for timeout seconds (more precisely, if no bytes have been received on the underlying socket for timeout seconds)."

So, while we will add a feature which handles socket timeouts in one way or another (to be determined), the 'total download time' version of timeout is actually not trivial.

I think printing "Equality check failed!" only to negate that statement in surrounding text is confusing, or at least distracting when glancing over the log output.
That's why my user-specified check descriptions (i.e. the string that a testcase author supplies to all my check functions) are just stating *what* is being checked. The check function then adds text saying whether the check passed or failed.

Example output would be
```
Pass    check_equal(): Checking 'First error counter' actual=0 expected=0
ERROR   check_equal(): Checking 'Second error counter' actual=1 expected=0 failed!
```
or
```
Pass   cmpUnsigned() 'ADC conversion value' actual=32256 expected=32255 error=1 (0.002% FS) maxerror=2621
```

As you can see we have "Pass" as an extra "log level", yes. That makes it also easier to postprocess the logs in order to add ANSI color sequences.

I see. This is somewhat a duplicate of https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/454. Checking an installation independently is possible, although, I'm not sure what the value would be. It would be very difficult to give a reasonable error message when it isn't satisfiable. I can think of some partial "solutions" to this problem, but the user experience would only be optimal with #454, I think. Otherwise it's really on the user to really make sure that things are "correct".

Also worth point out that there is already sort of a way to achieve this. `conda update --all` is equivalent to `conda install A B C ...` for all the all the packages installed in the environment. This will generate a consistant installation if it can, for the reason I noted [above](https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1268#issuecomment-91933348). This in general updates everything because conda updates packages when you install them. But you can keep packages downgraded by pinning them (http://continuum.io/blog/advanced-conda-part-1#pinning-packages). 

Checking pip packages is outside the scope of conda.

The simplest solution I have found is to move the newline from `PS1` to `PROMPT_COMMAND`:

```
bldred='\e[1;31m' # Red
bldcyn='\e[1;36m' # Cyan
bldwht='\e[1;37m' # White
txtrst='\e[0m'    # Text Reset - Useful for avoiding color bleed
    
PROMPT_COMMAND="printf '\n'"
export PS1="\[$bldred\]\u\[$txtrst\]@\[$bldwht\]\h\[$txtrst\]:\[$bldcyn\]\w\[$txtrst\]$ "
```

This allows `conda` to maintain it's default `PS1` behavior all while separating bash commands with newlines:

```
user@short-domain:fullpath$ source activate <env-name-here>
    
(<env-name-here>) user@short-domain:fullpath$
```

There's no doubt that avoiding thread locals is cleaner. The main arguments against them are:
1. They assume your code is always going to be run by a threaded server (probably true for zope, but not necessarily for people experimenting with async approaches) with only one request being served per thread.
2. They're not re-entrant (so, for example, one module being published by the Zope publisher can't call to publish a subrequest without fiddling with the thread locals to make sure they're restored afterward).
3. They make your code harder to test and run outside of the web framework, because you have to remember to set up and tear down the thread locals.

On the flip side, it's a nicer experience for the developer if they don't have to pass the request around everywhere.

What if we made a helper like this:

    from Testing.makerequest import makerequest
    from zope.globalrequest import getRequest
    import Zope2
    def get_request():
        request = getRequest()
        if request is None:
            request = makerequest(Zope2.app()).REQUEST
        return request
and use that in plone.api when it wants an implicit request? That way at least it doesn't break in tests and zopectl run scripts, addressing objection 2 above. (Might need to add some marker interfaces to the request from makerequest.)

But I think for the cases shown here, get_view and show_message, it's probably better to be explicit about it, since they are both actions that don't make much sense without a request. Actually in the case of show_message I wish it was just a method on the request so I didn't have to import anything, even from plone.api.

PR #67 solves this issue. Within that PR is a function, `pulp_smash.utils.get_broker`. It has the following signature:

def get_broker(server_config):
    """Build an object for managing the target system's AMQP broker."""
    return cli.Service(server_config, ...)  # either qpidd or rabbitmq

Another option for solving this problem is to create a subclass of `Service`, like so:

```python
class Broker(Service):
    def __init__(self, server_config):
        service = …  # 'qpidd', 'rabbitmq', etc.
        super(Broker, self).__init__(server_config, service)
```

However, this approach has two drawbacks:

* It favors inheritance over composition.
* It violates the Liskov substitution principle, due to its `__init__` method requiring a more specific set of arguments.

That said, the ultimate test of whether some code is good lies not in its theoretical prettiness, but whether it clearly and correctly accomplishes its job, and whether it is flexible enough to accomodate inevitable changes in requirements and usage patterns. And my opinion is not universally shared: @bmbouter favors the class-based approach.

On a related note, I'm trying to keep some level of separation between generic CLI-handling logic and Pulp-specific logic. The API and CLI handling code is, thus far, quite generic and could in theory by used by Robottelo or anyone else who wants some Python code for executing commands.

@QCoDeS/core 

Okay, this PR got a bit out of hand, but that's life sometimes. Three major changes are introduced here:

1. The database upgrade system changed and became "more correct". The `connect` function that creates the database takes an optional version argument. This means that the database is always created at version 0 and then upgrades are applied.

2. The `atomic` context manager now yields itself. This is needed to give nested context managers awareness about who is "on top". This *is* actually an API change, but it also fixes a bug, so I think it is acceptable. We now write 
```python
with atomic(conn) as conn:
    transaction(conn)
```

3. The config system will upon import check the `GUID_components` part of the config and prompt for an input if they are found to be zero.

While I understand the intention, I don't see why the tests should be
limited to MKLDNN. You want to test certain shapes that then trigger a
special behaviour in the MKLDNN backend and that's totally fine and the
best way to do it! But what I don't understand is why we can't run this
test on the default MXNet backend on CPU or GPU. What would happen? Would
it fail? If it wouldn't (and I hope that it won't), I don't see much reason
why this should be a MKLDNN specific test. We're basically increasing the
coverage of the test by moving it out of the MKLDNN specialised tests
without sacrificing anything. On the other hand, we're even making sure
that things, that users are supposed to do with the MKLDNN backend, also
works for other backends. The backend is and should be entirely invisible
to our users and thus we should always ensure (on a best effort base) that
our features work on all backends. We employ the same policy to CUDA: Every
test that can be run on CPU also has to pass on GPU. MKLDNN is, from my
point of view, just another type, thus making it CPU-MXNet, CPU-MKLDNN and
GPU-CUDA. Means, every backend has to pass the same set of tests - starting
with exceptions like this one leaves room for diverging functionality of
the different backends. In the end, our users will be facing a bad
calculation or an unexpected error and then leave with a bad user
experience - we want to avoid that at all costs.

> Is that syntax valid for RC1 or just RC2?

RC2, part of the move is unifying `dnu restore` and `nuget restore`. DNU will be gone by the time RC2 ships. NuGet currently supports "imports" but not DNU, here's an example https://github.com/dotnet/cli/blob/master/src/Microsoft.DotNet.ProjectModel.Workspaces/project.json#L14. in RC2, we're adding support to make this an array so you can specify multiple overrides. https://github.com/NuGet/Home/issues/1962

> If RC2, does that mean that there's no way to publish a net-core-targetting-pcl package in RC1 that can be consumed by an RC2 project without releasing a new version?

Correct. We want to keep netstandard clean, so it'll likely be a year or so before people can fully remove imports. This allows us to remove any backwards compat burden from the TFM and allows users to move forward with existing packages.

In terms of URI, I'm really mirroring aws-sdk here, where you have two ways (at least) to refer to an object:
- by bucket & key --- in our case by username, datasetid, and featureid
- by s3://bucket/key uri --- mapbox://datasets/username/datasetid/featureid

Following that model, `aws s3api` commands **always** require you to use one convention, while high-level `aws s3` commands **always** require you to use the other:

```
$ aws s3api get-object --bucket my-bucket --key my/key
$ aws s3 cp s3://my-bucket/my/key - 
```

So I'm inclined to only support full `mapbox://datasets/...` uris for high level functions, and continue with the individual arguments for API mirroring functions. I'm also inclined to use all the static prefixing due to precedence already set in the upload API, where uploads with a `mapbox://datasets/...` uri are interpreted to be dataset --> tileset generation requests. This is how `mapbox datasets create-tileset` works.

> are these more unixy variants intended to replace the more API-centric commands entirely? Or live side-by-side?

100% side-by-side. No intention of removing the functions that are straight mirrors of individual API calls.

> The username can be inferred through the access token and the prefixes are static.

Yes, but if somewhere down the road we were to allow for cross-account dataset reads, having built the username into the URI would allow updating the mapbox cli to be a non-breaking change.

Finally, for additional commands that don't require multiple API calls or any trickery, we still have the API mirror functions:

- mapbox datasets create
- mapbox datasets read-dataset DATASETID
- mapbox datasets update-dataset DATASETID

when `pi.vpred` is between in `oldpi.vpred` and ret, and the distance from `pi.vpred` to `oldpi.vpred` is large than `clip_param`, the pi.vpred will be clipped. 

for example, 
`oldpi.vpred < pi.vpred < ret, pi.vpred - oldpi.vpred > clip_param`
then, 
`pi.vpred -> oldpi.vpred + clip_param`

> It is possible, however, to have your data survive a interpreter shutdown and restart by having numpy operate on your own memory: pass a pointer to allocated memory (of at least the required size) into the py::array/py::array_t constructors. FWIW, in my experience, it seems that NumPy is not robust against restarting an interpreter. From how the C API is imported, I don't think there's any trigger for C extensions to recognize when a new instance of NumPy is loaded, so it *may* have stale API entries (e.g. for `pybind11`, `npy_api::get()` could be stale, if the solib is unloaded / then reloaded?). For performance considerations, we cannot afford to read each file to get the tags. It therefore only makes sense to use file system extended attributes to store tags. Here are the facts

1. Python 3.3 + supports [extended file attributes](https://python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/library/os.html) but only for Linux.
2. [`xattr`](https://github.com/xattr/xattr) supports both linux and mac.
3. windows supports such attributes, but it is very complicated and there is no Python library for it (as far as I know).
4. These attributes tend to get lost but `rsync` has limited support for it so there is a good chance that we can make it work within and between mac and linux.

It is unlikely that this feature will work very well in a cross-platform fashion but we might be able to get it working reasonably well locally (linux and mac)...

If you look in, say, `tests/test_wyoming.py`, you'll see how they generally work. We use a tool called VCRpy to record web responses and play them back during testing--avoiding the need to have network access and a working server to run the test. You'll call `@recorder.use_casette` and pass it a filename where it should look for the recorded response. The first time you run the test, the file won't exist, so it will make the actual remote request, and save the response. Future execution of the test will use the recorded file. From there just call your code and write a few asserts to validate that everything is working properly.

@avelis could you give a little more detail of your scenario?

There are a couple of ways Anymail could offer this:

1. As a *per-message* override, probably in the `esp_extra` dict. (Similar to how it handles [overriding Mailgun's sender_domain](https://anymail.readthedocs.org/en/latest/esps/mailgun/#email-sender-domain).) Something like:

    ```python
    message = EmailMessage(...)
    message.esp_extra = {"username": "OtherApiUser", "password": "..."}  # or "api_key": "..."
    message.send()
    ```

2. As a *connection-specific override,* which would apply to all emails sent using that connection:

    ```python
    message1 = EmailMessage(...)
    message2 = EmailMessage(...)
    connection = get_connection('anymail.backends.sendgrid.SendGridBackend',
                                username="OtherApiUser", password="...")  # or api_key="..."
    connection.send_messages([message1, message2])
    ```

The first approach is probably easier to explain and use. The second approach is (I think) more in keeping with the spirit of Django email backend connections, and is more likely to be portable to other ESPs and API bindings. (Which is why it would help to understand if this is a SendGrid-specific scenario or more generally applicable.)

> Perhaps this is an issue with Debian, Ubuntu, RHEL, Fedora, etc. If that's the case, I guess I should be filing tickets with them. In my mind having a LTS release that ships a broken version of openssl means that a critical piece of security software is not supported.

As far as they're concerned, OpenSSL "works" because few of their users are as conscious as those of us who work on this stuff and care. That's why their certificate package contains the old (soon to be) insecure certificates and no one cares. Because "working" is better than "secure" for their users and they understand that. Again, this isn't an immediate problem, but if you're planning on staying on 14.04 until 2019 (or later) expect this to be a very serious problem. At that point, updating the severely outdated versions of their packages that rely on openssl's C bindings (which have proven to be volatile between 1.0.0, 1.0.1, and 1.0.2) will be an incredible headache when this problem does strike (assuming enough of the population is still trusting 1024 bit roots which it will be if people stick to RHEL, Ubuntu, Debian, etc.).

> Their promise of LTS is then broken. I am honestly not sure, is this in fact the case? If so, shouldn't we all be screaming about this everywhere we can until the issue is somehow fixed?

They're promise of LTS is to patch show-stopping vulnerabilities. Problems with the kernel, etc. while providing stability. This isn't show-stopping because it hasn't been actively exploited yet (that we know about). Again, there have been issues on their trackers about this, and their solution is to default to less secure by default in this area.

> So where is the gain in security?

The gain in security is two-fold:

1. visibility. I suspect you've all learned a lot more about this than you ever wanted to know
1. Anyone starting with certifi for the first time will see this and know to update where possible.

> So Fedora found a process of tightening security without immediately breaking production environments for its users. As you said, they are waiting until openssl 1.0.2 is available before making it mandatory. Can certifi mimic this process?

They have an opt-in process through an esoteric command line call. That's not something certifi can mimic, plus that wouldn't be an opt-in for just one application, that's an opt-in for the entire system (all things importing and relying on certifi).

> However, having a more gradual rollout period would be better than not. http://certifi.io/en/latest/ has no mention of this change or how it will affect things.

Let's be honest, did you check that before updating the package? That's also not purely for python-certifi. That's for every language's certifi. Cory and I only control the Python one (for now). Updating that page would have implied an update for all packages which we have not yet done.

import numpy.ma as ma
def make_mosaic(imgs, nrows, ncols, border=1):
    """
    Given a set of images with all the same shape, makes a
    mosaic with nrows and ncols
    """
    nimgs = imgs.shape[0]
    imshape = imgs.shape[1:]
    
    mosaic = ma.masked_all((nrows * imshape[0] + (nrows - 1) * border,
                            ncols * imshape[1] + (ncols - 1) * border),
                            dtype=np.float32)
    
    paddedh = imshape[0] + border
    paddedw = imshape[1] + border
    for i in xrange(nimgs):
        row = int(np.floor(i / ncols))
        col = i % ncols
        
        mosaic[row * paddedh:row * paddedh + imshape[0],
               col * paddedw:col * paddedw + imshape[1]] = imgs[i]
    return mosaic

What has happened here:
 1. postgres started and failed quickly. Off course it removed postmaster.pid 
 2. `wait_for_port_open` was waiting 60 seconds, because it was not able to figure out that postmaster process is already dead (it doesn't know postmaster pid).

The original pg_ctl wait implementation calls `waitpid((pid_t) pm_pid, &exitstatus, WNOHANG)` every second and compares result with postmaster pid.
And here comes a tricky part...  Currently we are reaping dead processes before and after main HA loop, otherwise (if waitpid was called from sigchild_handler) subprocess was not getting the return code...

1. `asyncio.ensure_future()` creates a task is coroutine is passed.
2. `asyncio.wait()` calls `ensure_future()`.
3. `loop.run_until_complete()` calls `ensure_future()`.

Every `async def` is in asyncio is executed inside a task. There is no way to run async function without task context without weird tricks.
If aiobotocore has these tricks -- the library should be fixed.

The build problems are almost certainly a local to your machine issue; we haven't changed anything with the watchman build machinery in some time, and I just tried rebuilding and installing via homebrew just now; it worked fine.  Perhaps worth noting, although I doubt it is significant, I had an update to the xcode cli tools that got installed last night.

Separately from that, both brew and the FB deployment system are shipping 3.2.  We tagged 3.3 this week but there have been no changes to the service since then that might impact the "took too long to load" error.

However, we did release fb-watchman 1.0.0 to npm yesterday that switches the nodejs client for watchman from using JSON to use our [BSER](https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/bser.html) encoding which resolves a dramatic quadratic-wrt-size-of-response perf problem in a JSON stream module that we were using.  Perhaps that will quash that class of error? https://github.com/facebook/watchman/issues/113 has more on that.

My suggestion is to either let chef keep you on 3.2 or just do a `brew install watchman` and let brew download the bottled watchman binary.

I can't help you resolve your local build issue I'm afraid; I'm just not intimate enough with the FB specifics to give you helpful advice.

I'm seeing this as well. Are you on Postgres? If so, it appears the new use of [cursors in Postgres in Django 1.11](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/databases/#transaction-pooling-server-side-cursors) is the culprit. You will need to add [`'DISABLE_SERVER_SIDE_CURSORS': True`](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/settings/#std:setting-DATABASE-DISABLE_SERVER_SIDE_CURSORS) to each entry in your `DATABASES` setting to get the queries working again.

After digging around in the documentation for the device I found you can turn off terminal capability discovery by appending `+t` to username when authenticating. Filtering the sequencing wasn't the problem, I was already ignoring them, it was the delay after authentication as the device was waiting for a response to `1b 5b 39 39 39  39 42 1b 5a 20` before sending the command prompt.

Specifically the code, the expected output and the current output.

For now, you can try the already suggested linux commandline approach.

```
stty -F /dev/ttyS4 baudrate
cat /dev/ttyS4
```

If that doesn't go well, try removing some of the echo-s from the serial line

`stty -F /dev/ttyS4 baudrate -echo -echoe -echok -echoke -echonl`

Also, have a look at the caveats of using readline:

https://pyserial.readthedocs.io/en/latest/shortintro.html#readline

I'd like to revisit this. If a ctor raises and repr fails, sentry/raven adds additional noise to the logs which has little to do with the actual exception.

I could silence the `sentry.errors.serializer` logger, but that may hide other more legitimate issues, so I think inspecting the stack before calling repr as proposed above is a better solution.

I had the same issue and solved it by replacing 
**exc_info is not None**
with 
**'exc_info is not None and sorted(exc_info) != sorted((None, None, None))**
In what cases do you think it'd cause duplicate events?

Generally -1 on this approach as it opens a can of worms. e.g. what about environment? does it also get its own template tag? where do we stop?

I think we could provide a context processor which exposes the Sentry config, but it's pretty easy to also provide your own (which is what we do).

More importantly, I think it's rare that you would want JS and Python errors in the same project (we focus on a project being a service these days), so I think the applicability of this is fairly low.

Récap de la discussion IRL :

## Packaging

- S'orienter vers une installation via `pip` par défaut.
- Consommer des paquets versionnés et ne plus courir derrière les versions les plus récentes.
- Lire la documentation (@fpagnoux donnera le lien) sur le packaging pour comprendre comment l'automatiser.

> Note : une grande partie de la frustration sur le packaging provient de `baremes-ipp`.

## Gestion de branches

- Il faut être très discipliné sur la taille des PR et les rendre aussi atomiques que possible, pour les intégrer rapidement dans `master`.
- Pour s'aider à se discipliner, chacun est invité à donner du feedback sur la manière dont une PR aurait pu être plus finement découpée, afin de s'améliorer dans le temps.
- Pour s'aider à se discipliner, nous allons faire l'expérience d'ouvrir les PR dès la création des branches, pour voir si cela permet d'éviter de dériver. Si l'expérience n'est pas concluante, on abandonnera  :wink:

## Dette actuelle

Focalisation sur l'intégration de `baremes-ipp` dans `master` ASAP, avec pour objectif une intégration d'ici la fin de la semaine, et pour moyen une collaboration rapprochée entre @fpagnoux et @benjello. Go go go!!! 😄 💪

I find the cause of the error. It can be easily fixed.
The precision of pywt.integrate_wavelet must be increased
_cwt.py: lines 77-83

            precision = 10
            int_psi, x = integrate_wavelet(wavelet, precision=precision)
            step = x[1] - x[0]
            j = np.floor(
                np.arange(scales[i] * (x[-1] - x[0]) + 1) / (scales[i] * step))
            if np.max(j) >= np.size(int_psi):
                j = np.delete(j, np.where((j >= np.size(int_psi)))[0])

int_psi has a size of 1024. Thus for higher scales, np.max(j) >= np.size(int_psi) is true.

To fix this, precision has to be increased until np.max(j) >= np.size(int_psi) i sfalse.

This **does** prevent double loading as, in your example, if 2 threads attempt to load, one will block on acquiring the lock while the other loads the subjob. After the lock is released the first thread then checks if it has been loaded while it was waiting and return if so:

```
            # obtain a lock to make sure multiple loads of the same object don't happen
            with self._load_lock:

                # just make sure we haven't loaded this object already while waiting on the lock
                if index in self._cachedJobs:
                    return self._cachedJobs[index]
```

The benefit of doing it here is that you are only acquiring a lock when it's actually needed, not on every `getItem` call. Considering the previous issues we've had with deadlocks, I really want to minimise the chances of being hit by them again.

This looks great! I made a few changes:

* I used register_cmap from matplotlib.cm instead of pyplot (at @tacaswell's suggestion here at SciPy sprints)

It turns out the `PartialEq` on `ExecutionError` was used in this PR. Only in tests.

I believe that:
```rust
assert_eq!(extract_execute_response(operation), Err(ExecutionError::MissingFiles(vec![...])));
```

is sufficiently more succinct and clear than:
```rust
let error = match extract_execute_response(operation).expect_err("Wanted error") {
  ExecutionError::MissingFiles(files) => assert_eq!(files, vec![...]),
  other => { panic("Wanted MissingFiles but got {}", other),
}
```

or extracting a function to do the same (basically, manually implementing PartialEq), that deriving `PartialEq` is the right thing to do here.

It seems like you have run into a compatibility issue similar to #158. Maybe turbodbc is making an illegal assumption of how drivers should signal that no more data is there that seems to be satisfied with the integration databases that are used for development. That would require a closer look.

There's not too much to see at this function, I fear. The problem is at the C/C++ level and not fixable in Python alone. A C function is used to get strings of 8-byte characters. Since the ODBC API does not really enforce how these strings are encoded (UTF-8, Latin-1, Cyricllic, etc), turbodbc _assumes_ that most sane databases would use UTF-8. When converting this to a real Unicode string for Python, errors happen when the string is _not_ encoded with UTF-8. That is the case here. The fix is to use another function of the ODBC API that has a _somewhat_ defined encoding.

"Index" is a special word in SQL. It looks like it's a little more special in CockroachDB than in PostgreSQL here, so it's not getting quoted properly by SQLAlchemy. As a quick fix, it should work if you rename that column. For a more complete solution, we need to augment the list of keywords so SQLAlchemy will quote this name properly.

> One advantage of letsencrypt-auto is that you're always running the latest version of the client.

As Gentoo is mainly based on non-binary packages which need to be compiled, many packages have a "9999" version number in the repository, [just like Let's Encrypt does](https://packages.gentoo.org/packages/app-crypt/letsencrypt). These packages will pull the latest CVS/Git/et c. files and compile it. So "re-merging" those packages will get you the most recent, bleeding egde software, just like `letsencrypt-auto`. Ofcourse it's not 100% comparible with `letsencrypt-auto`, because Gentoo will obviously not get the most recent files on running `letsencrypt`, but will need a manual remerge of the package, but it's very much possible to get the most recent software without the need for `letsencrypt-auto` :wink:

An interesting question is how this might relate to testing network protocols. We already have some mechanisms in the memory-stream code to make tests where data trickles in on a weird schedule; one way to think of this is that when one task calls `send_all` and another is blocked in `receive_some`, there is a whole space of possible synchronization options there where some amount of the `send_all` becomes available to the `receive_some`, and maybe you want to re-use the same schedule exploration logic to explore those too.

Right now we use ad hoc randomized sleeps for this kind of thing, which is a bit cumbersome in general, and I especially struggled when writing the ssl tests to get them to give good coverage over all the weird scheduling/network interactions that can happen there. In particular one problem is that if you have one task that does 1 sleep, and another task that does 3 sleeps, then it's very rare that the second task will ever complete first, just because P(X) > P(X1 + X2 + X3) is not high when X, X1, X2, X3, are all equidistributed.

I am a new user of dateparser, and I was very disappointed to see that it doesn't take into account the language to guess the date format day/month/year vs month/day/year. For example:

```
>>> import dateparser
>>> dateparser.parse(u'2/12/2015', languages=['fr'])
datetime.datetime(2015, 2, 12, 0, 0)
```

In French, the day is always before the month, so the result should be datetime.datetime(2015, 12, 2, 0, 0). It's quite disappointing ; it means you ALWAYS have to give the date_formats as argument, otherwise you will have some big troubles !

@nkeim @danielballan When the user supplies a `raw_image` to `locate`, we can just as well separate out the characterization in a different function like `tp.characterize(refined_features, image)`. This will make it clearer to use in my opinion. As you would have to do the image loading and slicing twice anyway, the performance wouldn't suffer much.

@tacaswell I completely agree on the usefulness of the rolling average. There is no problem in feature finding and I think we shouldn't change that, also to keep functionality close to the IDL code. The only issue is that the mass & max signal are always underestimated, because you subtract an average that consists of the feature itself and the background.

The issue is related to the normalization that is described in the original Corcker&Grier paper, eqn. 3 and 4. We don't do this normalization (at least for the rolling average we don't do it, for the gaussian convolution, I am not sure).
The error in our apparent mass is approx. M_apparent = M (1 - Nw/Ns), Nw the number of pixels inside the binary mask and Ns the number of pixel in the rolling average box. I investigated this a bit in [this notebook](http://nbviewer.ipython.org/urls/dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/36853001/Processed%20mass%20deviation.ipynb) but I think that this should be solved inside the preprocessing and not afterwards like I did in that notebook.

Increasing the `smoothing_size` is no option, it is already by default `2*diameter+1` (so `2*(2*radius+1)+1`, I think this is a discrepancy between Crocker&Grier). Larger `smoothing_size` will give you enormous margins and issues with closely spaced features. Decreasing `smoothing_size` will increase the error in mass&signal (and therefore in epsilon).

Summarizing:

- For this PR, I think adding a new column `raw_mass` and fixing the rescale issue with `mass` and `signal` will solve most of the issues
- A new issue should be started in which the normalization is investigated
- A new issue in which the characterization on raw image should be facilitated

Though you can also just forward events on ManticoreEVM.load(). 
```
    def load(self, state_id=None):
        ''' Load one of the running or final states.
            
            :param state_id: If None it assumes there is a single running state
            :type state_id: int or None
        '''
        state = None
        if state_id is None:
            #a single state was assumed
            if self.count_running_states() == 1:  
                #Get the ID of the single running state              
                state_id = self._running_state_ids[0]
            else:
                raise Exception("More than one state running.")

        if state_id == -1:
            state = self.initial_state
        else:
            state = self._executor._workspace.load_state(state_id, delete=False)
            #froward events from newly loaded object
            self._executor.forward_events_from(state, True)
        return state
```
This way the events will be forwarded even if the state is loaded by the user outside the mainloop.

This is definitely our most common feature request, and it's the biggest doubt I have about whether we made the right design choices with peru. The short answer is that peru really isn't designed for that workflow, but the `override` feature exists to kinda sorta let you do that. Have you tried it?

Here's the long answer:

Peru is designed to run every time, inside whatever build/run script you have at the top of your project. The goal is that the human shouldn't need to *remember* any special build steps. This is important when you bounce around the history of your project, or when you pull new commits. Did your dependencies change? Do you need to sync again? By running peru every time, you never need to ask that question. Your build always does the Right Thing.

That goal means that peru needs to have substantial control over the files it puts on disk. It works a lot like `git checkout` in this way, and in fact, it uses git under the covers. When you run `peru sync` with new dependencies, peru will change existing files and clean up files that don't need to be there anymore. That's convenient (and more importantly, correct!) for dependencies that you never touch. But it means that peru needs to be cautious to avoid wiping out any changes you might've made. It'll raise an error if it sees that any files have changed since you last synced them.

And *that* goal in turn means that it's difficult to make peru play nicely with the workflow you described. Imagine peru cloned a git repo for you and put it directly in your project. As soon as you started editing files in that repo, peru would freak out and break your build. You'd need to switch to a workflow where you only called peru during setup or something, at which point you might as well just use git submodules. The `override` feature was designed to deal with this. It lets you change the *source* of one of your modules, to refer to your clone somewhere else on disk. The downsides are that you need to run `peru sync` in your main project every time you make a change in the module (though you should do that automatically anyway), and that the clones have to be side-by-side or something instead of nested.

So that's the Big Reason peru hasn't done this. There are side benefits, like the `pick` and `export` features, and being able to import the contents of two modules into the same directory, none of which would be possible with real clones. There are also performance concerns. Since peru expects to run every time, a cached sync should be very fast. We get that by storing all your imports as one big git tree, so that syncing in the best case boils down to a single `git status`. But git trees are not allowed to store a `.git` folder in themselves (for [security reasons](http://article.gmane.org/gmane.linux.kernel/1853266)), so this design is incompatible with laying out real git clones, at least without some serious hacks.

So yeah, that's probably more than you wanted to know :) I'm interested to hear more of your thoughts, especially about `override`.

The temporary files are in `/tmp` or wherever you specify with `-t`. They are deleted on creation so they are only a nameless inode most of the time, but you can see their original names in `/proc/*/fd` if you look.

The memory seems fairly believable. During parsing, the only thing that is in memory is the JSON parse tree itself; the rest is being streamed to disk.

The intensive part is immediately after that ("Reordering geometry"), where it maps the geometry file into memory and writes it out to a new temporary file ordered by quadtree index instead of the input order. This can thrash a lot because it is not sequential at all unless the original data was in close to that order. That's probably where you saw 8.5GB of memory used, and I should do a new release because it could be smaller than 46 bytes now.

Then the tiling part is basically sequential: reading from temporary files and writing to new temporary files, and at each moment having only the features that will actually appear in the final output of a single tile in memory at any one moment. It is not a very efficient format in memory (64-bit drawing instructions, 64-bit coordinates) but I didn't try to crunch it because it's only a tile at a time.

The time seems fairly believable. Parsing GeoJSON is slow because JSON is complicated. I should make another input format that doesn't spend so much time on punctuation.

Hello @adarvit , the `depth_point` is basically a 3D coordinate in metric units with the origin located at the sensor's base. See [Librealsense reference](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/wiki/Projection-in-RealSense-SDK-2.0#point-coordinates) for more details.

As for the code -  it seem legit.  I'm not sure I understand the question, but if you want to verify the data flow you may check the following:
>depth_frame = frames.get_depth_frame()

If the input is a depth-aligned-to-color frame, then its intrinsic and the color's frame intrinsic must be identical.

You haven't specified what was the original depth value at  [100,100] before deprojecting the pixel, nor the 'depth_scale'. And while the results you've got are legit for a particular case (the 3D point is located very close, yet in front of the camera) it is not possible to deduce more from this.

Try several distinct (and valid) depth coordinates - deproject them to see how they correspond to the scene. One quick assert is that de-projection must not modify the Z (depth) value, only multiply by `depth_scale`

We are using a sub class of `graphene.Field` to handle this sort of thing.

```python
class CustomModelField(Field):
    """
    A subclass of graphene's builtin Field.
    We automatically find non-id unique attributes and allow those
    as arguments to the given node type.

    Usage:
        entity = CustomModelField(EntityGrapheneType)
    """

    def __init__(self, field_type, **kwargs):
        self.field_type = get_type(field_type)

        unique_fields = self.get_unique_fields(_type=field_type)

        is_required = len(unique_fields) == 1

        graphql_args = {}
        for model_field in unique_fields:
            form_field = model_field.formfield() or CharField()
            form_field.required = is_required
            graphene_field = convert_form_field(form_field)
            graphql_args[model_field.name] = graphene_field

        all_kwargs = graphql_args
        all_kwargs.update(kwargs)

        super().__init__(
            field_type,
            **all_kwargs
        )

    def get_unique_fields(self, _type=None):
        if _type:
            model = self.get_model_from_type(_type)
        else:
            model = self.model

        unique_fields = [f for f in model._meta.local_fields if f.unique]
        return unique_fields

    def model_resolver(self, root, args, context, info):
        if not args:
            unique_field_names = [f.name for f in self.get_unique_fields()]
            err_msg = 'At least one of {} is required.'.format(unique_field_names)
            raise GraphQLError(err_msg)

        qs = optimize_queryset(self.manager, info)

        return self.model._default_manager.get(**args)

    def get_resolver(self, parent_resolver):
        return self.model_resolver
```

I cut out a bit for brevity to just show the idea. Basically, we automatically add arguments based on any unique fields on the model. So from your example, it'd just be:

```python
class Query(graphene.AbstractType):
    lesson = CustomModelField(LessonType)
```

Then the `id` would be added as an argument, and the resolver would just use the ORM to pass the unique fields to `default_manger.get`.

That means if the user does `sudo /usr/bin/python -m pip install afdko`, they will go to `/usr/bin` where the system python is, or if they use the homebrew python or the Python.org python (which don't need to `sudo`), the scripts will go to the global `/usr/local/bin`, or if they pass `--user` option to pip install, the scripts will go to the user's scripts folder (on Linux `~/.local/bin/`, on Mac ~/Library/Python/X.Y/bin (I think?), Windows somewhere else in the user's profile folders), or finally if installing inside a virtual environment, they'll go to the venv's bin or Scripts folder.

It's important that all these scenarios are supported (like with a regular python package) and that you don't make any assumptions about users wanting the scripts, e.g., in /usr/bin.

I'd argue that that is the worse location for them to be. That is a location reserved for operating system tools, not for user-installed scripts. Any OS update can wipe those out, or they may risk breaking other OS features.

I strongly recommend not to rely on the system python but install a third-party python distribution (if you don't want to use homebrew or compile youself with pyenv, then just grab the official GUI installers from python.org).

I've just had a similar problem with an Integer-field. How would you solve the task to show two different messages for these two cases:

1. The user enters nothing, which means the browser will submit an empty string. That means you want to show a message like "Please enter a number."

2. The user enters something that is not a valid number, e.g. `"12abc&$"`. You would like to show an error-message along the lines of "Only number-values are allowed."

Currently I don't see a nice way to distinguish missing input from invalid numerical input, because marshmallow treats the empty string as non-missing. This is not a problem for JSON-submitted data (e.g. from Angular, ...) of course. But for standard browser forms that are POSTed, this will be relevant.

* Drawbacks - too many to list.
* General suggestion is that pymatpro should be a *thin* layer. Everything that can be "outsourced" to public packages should be so. If pymatgen-db already has a db access, improve on that rather than writing your own in pymatpro. If builders need a "workflow" style process, use fireworks. The more code is public, the more eyes will look at the code. The reason why pymatpro is the way it is today is because privacy promotes laziness. There are no tests, people write all sorts of things badly, and no one ever looks at them.
* Dependencies - use as many public, well-maintained code bases as you like. The more, the better.

"bbdata.fits" contains the full images and other info.  They are in common
broadband HST and JWST filters.  These have no astronomical noise nor PSF.
There is additional documentation in the FITS headers, and please let me
know if there's a parameter or detail you need to know; I've copied the
unit conversion I use most often below.  We estimated catalogs of the
intrinsic redshifts, masses, etc as a function of position for at least one

want me to post these, too -- I'll need to do some minor work to remind
myself about this.  You can also estimate redshifts of objects using the
images and the HDUs called "zweightslice" or "weighted_z" which contain the
brightness-weighted object redshift in each pixel.  Similarly the stellar
masses from "stellar_mass" HDUs.  As examples, "bbslice_0004.fits" contains
the full data for only z~1-1.5  (there are 10 total such slices available
per field); and "broadband_0033.fits" contains the full data for only

The 4096x4096 pixel images are 0.0416178 arcsec/pixel, are fully
extragalactic, include all simulated stellar populations at z < 18
(practically speaking, z ~< 12 I think), and contain no observatory
effects.  I used Bruzual & Charlot (2003) stellar pops, and assumed the
baseline Charlot & Fall (2000) dust model. They are in surface brightness
units:  W/m/m^2/Sr, which I most often convert to Jy using something like:
F [in Jy] = 1e26 * ((effective wavelength)^2)/c * (pixel size in
steradians) * Original Image [in W/m/m^2/Sr]
F [in Jy] = 1e14 * (effective wavelength in microns)^2 *  (0.0416/206265)^2
/ (3e8)  * Original Image [in W/m/m^2/Sr]
F [in Jy] = 1.356e-8 * (effective wavelength in microns)^2 * Original Image

First of all, I'd like to set out what I believe to be an illustrative and realistic usage scenario:

- The application has a number of replicated instances.
- Each instance sends custom metrics to the service once a minute. It only sends one point per time series.
- Each metric is identified by several labels. These might, for example, represent different categories of requests being processed, and different response statuses. There might be tens or hundreds of label combinations. Let's just assume that each instance writes them all each time. It writes them in just one request (or a few, if there are more than 200) because that's quicker and uses less request quota. That also enables the service to operate more efficiently.
- The monitored resource (the type and all the labels) is the same for all of those time series, for a given instance. The type might be `gce_instance`, `gke_container`, or `aws_ec2_instance`, as the case may be. (If the application were not replicated, one might use the `global` monitored resource type instead.)

Some thoughts:

- Batching of multiple time series in a single write is important.
- Batching of multiple points for the same time series is not important. (The API might allow that someday, for special situations where it is useful.)
- It would indeed make sense to allow the application to specify the monitored resource just once at startup. That said, I don't think it's worth introducing a class just for this reason.
- It does not make sense to try to factor out the metric in the same way, at least not in a simple client library that wraps the API straightforwardly.

I would reiterate the proposal I made in [#1867](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/issues/1867#issuecomment-229784134):

```
# Write a point to a time series.
client.write_point(metric=metric, resource=resource, value=3.14) # API call

# Write data to several time series. The argument is a sequence of TimeSeries objects.
client.write_time_series([ts1, ts2, ..., tsN]) # API call
```

I don't see value in messing with metric descriptors when writing custom metric data. We can just deduce the value type from the Python type of the value. That will mean that one can't provide an `int` when the value type is `DOUBLE`, or a `float` when the value type is `INT64`, but so be it.

I believe ```ShapeValidator.get_data_shape``` should already support this, however, for generators it will need to load the first generated value to determine the shape, which may be problematic here. I think the correct fix may be a bit later in the DataChunkIterator.__init__

```
        # If we still don't know the shape then try to determine the shape from the first chunk
        if self.max_shape is None and self.__next_chunk.data is not None:
            data_shape = ShapeValidator.get_data_shape(self.__next_chunk.data)
            self.max_shape = list(data_shape)
            self.max_shape[0] = None
            self.max_shape = tuple(self.max_shape)
```

Instead of always setting ```self.max_shape[0]=None``` here, we should probably check if ``len`` is supported by the iterator and set the first dimensions of max_shape accordingly.

Hello @annam30 

Thank you for sharing your issue.

The reference doesn't appear to be violating the spec, as your tools have an empty entry for `baseCommand`:
```
        "baseCommand": [
          ""
        ]
```

According to http://www.commonwl.org/v1.0/CommandLineTool.html#Input_binding the last step to creating the command line is "[to i]nsert elements from baseCommand at the beginning of the command line."

[Your Docker image](https://hub.docker.com/r/avkitex/wget/) has `ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/bin/wget"]`. In CWL we don't use Docker containers with entry points, as the CWL implementations create the command line from the tool definition.

I recommend removing the `ENTRYPOINT` from your Docker image and changing your CWL definition to have `baseCommand: wget` (or `"baseCommand": [ "wget" ]` to match your formatting)
